findings.title: Real creator + AI clone, kept on OnlyFans
findings.claim: Cloning a real signed creator into an AI model and mixing AI with real content lets an agency keep OnlyFans conversion while multiplying volume.
findings.what_they_do: Take 20-30 photos of a real signed creator, train an AI model on her face/body, generate motion-control videos, then post a real+AI mix that all funnels to her real OnlyFans (no AI sold past the wall). One model films 35-40 videos/week; AI fills the other 40-45, enabling 4+ accounts.
findings.vs_us: Palm already runs a recreation pipeline for AI personas funneling to OnlyFans (§3), but does NOT yet clone REAL creators to scale their social volume. This is a direct extension of Palm's existing AI-persona capability onto the real-creator core business (§0/§1).
findings.recommendation: Pilot cloning one cooperative real creator: train an AI likeness, post a real/AI mix to her existing funnel, keep all OF vault content 100% real. Leverage Palm's existing recreation pipeline.

sources.quote: you take a real creator and you generate your own AI profile of that creator

findings.title: Hybrid keeps OnlyFans trust vs full-AI Fanvue conversion loss
findings.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
findings.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.
findings.vs_us: Confirms Palm's core OnlyFans funnel as the high-conversion asset (§0). Palm's AI personas use OnlyFans-via-social, so this validates Palm's choice not to go Fanvue-only.
findings.recommendation: Hold the OnlyFans funnel as the conversion anchor for both real and AI lines; treat Fanvue-style platforms only as fallback, never primary.

sources.quote: Your conversion rates will literally drop 30 to 50% just by doing that platform rather than Only Fans

findings.title: 20% real / 80% AI mix to keep trust and volume
findings.claim: A roughly 20% real and 80% AI content mix maximizes posting volume while keeping enough real proof that viewers trust the creator is a real person.
findings.what_they_do: Posts ~20% real creator content and ~80% AI; warns that 90-95% AI makes fans think the page is fake, while pin posts plus a recent real story verify authenticity. Recommends 30-50% real in week one to build trust faster.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a mature data-driven social pipeline (§1) but no codified real-vs-AI blend ratio for a hybrid creator. This gives a concrete starting ratio.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a default ~20-30% real / 70-80% AI blend for hybrid creators, front-loading real content during launch; codify the ratio in the SMM content spec.

sources.quote: 20% real content kept people trusting it

findings.title: Strip AI metadata before posting or reach is throttled
findings.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
findings.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.
findings.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona program is built for compliance (EU AI Act label + watermark, §3), which is the opposite posture. This is a competitor risk tactic Palm should note but weigh against its compliance stance.
findings.recommendation: Note as a reach lever for non-EU/unlabeled experiments only; do NOT apply to Palm's EU-AI-Act-labeled personas. Flag the compliance tension for leadership.

sources.quote: there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

findings.title: Motion control lets you copy trends in minutes
findings.claim: Generating AI versions of trending videos via motion control lets an agency post a trend within minutes instead of waiting days for a creator to film.
findings.what_they_do: Downloads a trending reference video, feeds it plus the AI model image into Cling motion control, and produces the creator's version of the trend in ~5-10 minutes to ride the wave while it is still hot.
findings.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) identifies WHAT to film from viral data but still depends on the creator filming. Motion control collapses the produce-time gap Palm currently has.
findings.recommendation: Add a motion-control fast-path to the inspo pipeline so promoted trend reels can be AI-rendered for hybrid creators within the trend window.

findings.title: Main brand account fed by disposable burner accounts
findings.claim: On X, a protected main brand account funneled by several disposable high-volume burner accounts maximizes growth while insulating the asset from bans.
findings.what_they_do: Runs one main account (2-3 quality posts/day, funnels to OF) plus 3-5 burner accounts (3-4 aggressive trend posts/day) that spam trends and pin replies funneling all traffic to the main; if a burner is banned, it's disposable.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic/distribution strategy for X (§5 gap). This is a concrete account-architecture pattern Palm lacks entirely.
findings.recommendation: Pilot a main+burner X structure for one creator; treat burners as expendable growth engines and the main as the OF-converting asset.

findings.title: Engagement-trap, trendjack, and paid promo pages on X
findings.claim: X growth is driven by reply-baiting engagement traps, fast trendjacking, and paying large promo pages to seed content with mystery hooks.
findings.what_they_do: Posts reply-bait ('describe me in one word'), jumps trends like Grok/Epstein image gimmicks within 15-30 min, and pays promo pages (100-500k followers) $50-200/post that hook viewers into clicking through to the creator's pinned reply.
findings.vs_us: Palm's traffic strategy is undocumented (§5 gap). These are specific paid + organic X tactics with no Palm baseline.
findings.recommendation: Capture as build-out menu for an X traffic SOP: engagement-trap caption library, daily trend monitoring, and a tested promo-page budget per creator.

sources.quote: the content designed to get replies

findings.title: Girlfriend-experience parasocial selling, not PPV spam
findings.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
findings.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.
findings.vs_us: Chatting is OUTSOURCED at Palm with no in-house SOP (§8). This is direct build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house and AI chat teams.
findings.recommendation: Capture the GFE framework (wall optimization, staged scripts, verification, no-spam rule) as the seed SOP for Palm's future in-house/AI chatting.

sources.quote: the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

findings.title: Track chatting ratio; target 1:9-1:10
findings.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
findings.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).
findings.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data (§9) but has no documented chatting KPI like this. The ratio is a clean leadership metric Palm could adopt immediately even while chatting is outsourced.
findings.recommendation: Have the outsourced chat team report a weekly chatting ratio per creator now, and bake the metric into Palm's whale-hunting/Chat Team Report views.

sources.quote: add up your messages and tips as well and divide it by your subscriptions

findings.title: Three chatters + reliever for seamless 24/7 shifts
findings.claim: Per-creator chatting is staffed with three eight-hour chatters plus one reliever, with documented handoffs for seamless 24/7 coverage.
findings.what_they_do: Runs 3 chatters (six days) + 1 reliever (three days), all 8-hour shifts; Discord clock-in/out, end-of-shift reports, and at-next-chatter handoff messages so in-progress conversations transfer without gaps.
findings.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting and has no internal shift model (§8). This is a concrete staffing template for the future in-house team.
findings.recommendation: Adopt the 3+1 eight-hour shift model with Discord clock-in/EOS reports as the staffing blueprint when Palm builds its in-house chat team.

findings.title: Revenue-gated hiring ladder: VAs, then chat manager, then ops
findings.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
findings.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.
findings.vs_us: Palm's org is thin with no documented hiring criteria, comp bands, or scaling plan (§10). This is a ready-made staffing/comp ladder.
findings.recommendation: Use this ladder as a template for Palm's org-scaling plan; map Palm roles (Amin, editors, chat) onto the stages and define revenue gates for next hires.

findings.title: Loom-video SOPs to eliminate training misinterpretation
findings.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
findings.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.
findings.vs_us: Palm has SOP-style workflows in the editor/warmup UIs but no documented Loom-first training standard for new hires (§10). Cheap, high-leverage practice.
findings.recommendation: Standardize Loom-video SOPs for every Palm role (editor, poster, warmup operator, future chatters); store alongside the existing playbook editor.

findings.title: Bans driven by unbranded sexual content, not IP/proxies
findings.claim: Instagram bans in OFM are driven mainly by unfocused overtly-sexual content and low trust score, not by IP/proxy/link setup as most operators assume.
findings.what_they_do: After testing 50+ accounts, concludes content (half-naked, unbranded, unfocused) is the highest-weight ban trigger; uses normal iPhone + real SIM/hotspot, no VPN/proxy, and builds a branded persona instead of bikini spam.
findings.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is hardware-isolated with proxies/device separation (§3/§4); for REAL creators Palm has no documented IG ban-avoidance posture (§4 gap). This reframes the real-creator risk as content-led.
findings.recommendation: For real-creator IG, prioritize branded non-explicit content and normal-user behavior over technical spoofing; document a real-creator ban-avoidance SOP.

sources.quote: 90% of bans initiate due to your content having zero focus

findings.title: Warm accounts 2 weeks and post before adding the OF link
findings.claim: New social accounts should be warmed as normal users and post content for about two weeks before adding the OnlyFans link to protect trust score.
findings.what_they_do: Acts as a normal user (scroll/like, follow niche on day 2, don't post day 1), waits ~2 weeks of posting before adding any link, and pre-builds accounts before signing a client so revenue never stalls.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a detailed 90-day AI warmup with a Day-45 OF-CTA gate (§4) but no formalized REAL-creator warmup (§4 gap). The 2-week pre-link rule is a lighter real-creator analogue.
findings.recommendation: Formalize a real-creator IG warmup: pre-build accounts, post ~2 weeks before link drop; reuse the AI warmup UI's gating concept for real accounts.

sources.quote: post content for 2 weeks before adding a link

findings.title: Branded persona replacing pure volume in 2026
findings.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
findings.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.
findings.vs_us: Palm's pipeline optimizes for viral content ideation (§1) but persona/branding is not a codified input. This argues brand should be a first-class field upstream of content selection.
findings.recommendation: Add a defined persona/brand brief to creator onboarding and feed it into the inspo pipeline so promoted ideas reinforce a consistent identity.

findings.title: Hybrid AI makes salary creators highly profitable
findings.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
findings.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.
findings.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented pricing/commission MODEL or salary-vs-split rationale (§7 gap). This is a concrete alternative deal structure.
findings.recommendation: Evaluate a salary-creator + AI-volume deal structure as an alternative to revenue split for select creators; document the margin math in a pricing model.

findings.title: Snapchat as an under-saturated OF traffic + income channel
findings.claim: Snapchat is an under-exploited OFM channel that simultaneously drives OF subs, cross-promotes other platforms, and pays direct creator monetization.
findings.what_they_do: Sets up a real-looking account, posts ~5 spotlights/day plus 30-100 clickbait story snaps/day (repurposed via repurpose.io or a VA), CTAs only once a week to avoid disabling, and uses paid promoted-snap boosts; cites a creator earning as much from Snapchat as from Instagram.
findings.vs_us: Palm's distribution is IG/TikTok via Publer + Telegram (§5); Snapchat is absent from Palm's stack. New channel with no Palm baseline.
findings.recommendation: Pilot Snapchat for one high-output creator: repurpose existing content, weekly-only CTA, test promoted snaps; measure as a third revenue lever.

findings.title: Central account/team/finance trackers to remove owner from ops
findings.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
findings.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.
findings.vs_us: Palm is already strong here — Airtable HQ + Content Ops, editor task queue, invoicing, whale CRM (§1/§7/§9). Validates Palm's systemization; the gap is a unified creator-phase/KPI rollup.
findings.recommendation: Add a single creator-phase + KPI rollup (revenue, chatting ratio, follower growth, account status) across Palm's Airtable bases, surfaced in the SMM hub Overview.
